coredata.dc:title: Tangerine peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots incorporated chitosan/pullulan-based active packaging film for bread packaging

coredata.dc:description: Citrus peel waste carbon dots based on nitrogen-doped (N-TanCD) were developed by a hydrothermal strategy to deliver active packaging fillers and characterized by transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence, and Fourier transform infrared analyses. The addition of N-TanCD into chitosan-pululan (CS/Pul@N-TanCD) polymer blend amplified the tensile strength of the composite film by 22.8 %, whereas the antioxidant activities against DPPH and ABTS reached 62.7 % and 91.6 %, respectively. The proposed film showed blocked 98.8 % of UV-A and 100 % of UV-B without affecting the film’s transparency. The CS/Pul@N-TanCD film lowered the contamination of L. monocytogenes and E. coli by more than 4 and 5 log CFU/mL, respectively. Sliced bread was packaged using CS/Pul-based films and stored for 12 days at 50 % relative humidity and 25 °C to investigate changes in the quality of the bread. It was found that bread packaged with CS/Pul film integrated with N-TanCD maintained excellent bread quality relating to appearance, moisture content, hardness, weight loss, and total viable bacterial count.

head.ce:title: Tangerine peel-derived nitrogen-doped carbon dots incorporated chitosan/pullulan-based active packaging film for bread packaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Food and Nutrition, BioNanocomposite Research Center, Kyung Hee University, 26 Kyungheedae-ro, Dongdaemun-gu, Seoul 02447, South Korea

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Citrus peel waste carbon dots based on nitrogen-doped (N-TanCD) were developed by a hydrothermal strategy to deliver active packaging fillers and characterized by transmission electron microscopy, photoluminescence, and Fourier transform infrared analyses. The addition of N-TanCD into chitosan-pululan (CS/Pul@N-TanCD) polymer blend amplified the tensile strength of the composite film by 22.8 %, whereas the antioxidant activities against DPPH and ABTS reached 62.7 % and 91.6 %, respectively. The proposed film showed blocked 98.8 % of UV-A and 100 % of UV-B without affecting the film’s transparency. The CS/Pul@N-TanCD film lowered the contamination of L. monocytogenes and E. coli by more than 4 and 5 log CFU/mL, respectively. Sliced bread was packaged using CS/Pul-based films and stored for 12 days at 50 % relative humidity and 25 °C to investigate changes in the quality of the bread. It was found that bread packaged with CS/Pul film integrated with N-TanCD maintained excellent bread quality relating to appearance, moisture content, hardness, weight loss, and total viable bacterial count.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), zero-dimensional carbon-based nanostructures, have secured great consideration because of their unique photoluminescence properties, favorable biocompatibility, excellent water solubility, and ease of preparation. These characteristics have led to CDs (<10 nm) being exploited in a variety of applications, such as bioimaging, drug delivery, photovoltaic devices, medical diagnostics, catalysis, sensing, and solar thermal conversion [1]. Additionally, it has been widely investigated that CDs are widely used as functional food components in films and coatings owing to their low toxicity, decent chemical stability, good compatibility, surface modification, and strong antioxidant and antibacterial activities [2,3]. In particular, biomass-derived CDs are generally recognized as environmentally safe alternatives that can provide the functional properties required for the manufacture of sustainable active packaging films [4,5].

On the other hand, food waste and food processing byproducts and their disposal are emerging as a major problem worldwide [6]. For example, with a yearly manufacturing of over 100 million tons, citrus fruits (Citrus reticulata) account for the largest share of global fruit production. However, byproducts generated during general food processing, such as citrus peels, account for approximately 20 % of the total weight and are causing some side effects, such as polluting the environment [6]. Therefore, developing sustainable, functional fillers (such as CDs) from the tangerine peel (which is usually decomposed) can not only be realized as functional filler for active packaging purposes but also contribute to waste management. Utilizing tangerine peel waste can help to reduce the amount of waste that would otherwise end up in landfills. This strategy can reduce the negative environmental impacts of waste disposal while promoting efficient use of resources.

Furthermore, citrus peels are thought to be a potential source of functional ingredients because they are abundant in bioactive substances, including flavonoids, limonoids, phenolic compounds, and polysaccharides [7]. These functional ingredients exhibit powerful anti-inflammatory, antioxidant, and anti-cancer characteristics [8,9]. Evaluating profitable products employing food waste contributes to environmental protection and food recycling. Therefore, citrus peels can be a useful ingredient in making green CDs because they are rich in carbon sources, have excellent biocompatibility, and have low toxicity. In particular, the outstanding electron donor and electron acceptor properties of CDs confer strong antioxidant properties to CDs [10]. CDs also offer brilliant UV shielding potential due to the abundance of π-π conjugated sites in their core [11]. In addition, CDs have strong antibacterial, photoelectric, and photothermal abilities, which are accredited due to the generation of ROS [12].

Currently, several biopolymer-based materials combined with nanoparticles are being employed for the conservation of numerous food products in the form of coatings and films for intelligent and active packaging applications [13,14]. Natural polymers such as proteins and polysaccharides have gained much consideration in the food packaging sector as green alternatives to petroleum-based synthetic packaging materials and films because of their low cost, biodegradable, non-toxic, and eco-friendly aptitudes [15,16]. Biopolymer-based packaging films have attracted much consideration as an alternative to petroleum-based polymers despite their drawbacks, such as poor gas barrier properties and low strength. Combining biopolymers to produce composites or blending films with functional nanofillers or bioactive chemicals is a significant approach to addressing the shortcomings of biobased polymer backbones [17,18].

In contrast, pullulan (Pul) is often used as a gelling agent and stabilizer due to its high hydroxyl structure, which contributes to its good hydrophilicity. In addition, pullulan has been applied as a food packaging material due to its unique qualities such as excellent film-forming ability, biocompatibility, biodegradability, non-mutagenic quality and gas barrier quality [19]. However, this polymer has limited mechanical properties and relatively high hydrophilicity [20]. This problem can be solved by combining pullulan with additional biopolymers. In this regard, the composition of chitosan and pullulan can be a suitable choice for solid-based polymer composites. Next to cellulose, chitosan (CS) is the most widespread biopolymer in nature and is well known as a reliable choice for food packaging applications due to its unique antimicrobial activity against various microorganisms and its non-toxicity, biocompatibility and degradability [17]. CS and Pul (CS/Pul) are two edible natural polymer polysaccharides that are well known for their excellent film-forming ability. Additionally, published studies have shown that this combination produces highly compatible films that compensate for the imperfections of each matrix [20,21].

Nowadays, due to the pressure of busy lifestyles, people prefer quick-cooking products or simple, less time-consuming cooking methods. In this context, baked sliced bread is observed as a basic food product due to its wide availability, easy transportation, lightweight, easy production method, satisfactory properties, low cost and sufficient nutritional value [22]. Therefore, although it has a limited shelf life, it is one of the major and vital staple foods. Rapid microbial decomposition due to bread spoilage or short shelf life is a major cause of bread waste. Prolonging the bread’s shelf life using appropriate packaging materials and packaging techniques is one of the best ways to prevent bread waste [23]. Packaging technology can be used to minimize harmful sources such as increased moisture content, increased microbial activity, enzymes, air, and temperature [24]. Therefore, packaging issues are important and must be taken seriously, as they have a great impact on preserving product quality and effective marketing of packaged goods. Currently, as consumers become more health conscious, the use of chemical preservatives is increasingly restricted. In this sense, sustainable packaging technologies must be used to meet the necessities of the “green” consumer market and extend the shelf life of bakery items without harmful chemicals [25]. Accordingly, cutting-edge skills such as active packaging are increasingly being researched in the food field. Active packaging refers to the purposeful use of components that interact with the packaging's inside environment while preserving the product's quality and safety. [4]. Many of these, however, remain in the research and development stage and have not yet reached full commercialization. Therefore, this work aims to fabricate functional food packaging materials by preparing multi-functional, eco-friendly fluorescent CDs using unused food waste resources. For this purpose, nitrogen-doped CDs were synthesized using tangerine peels, and their fluorescence properties, antioxidant efficacy in ABTS and DPPH analysis, and antibacterial activity against four types of bacteria and two types of fungi were evaluated. CD structures can be modified by doping heteroatoms into the core carbon structure to obtain desired functional properties, broadening the scope of CD utilization in various applications. It has been previously demonstrated that doping modifies the electronic band gap between energy levels and stabilizes the final structure by forming bonds between heteroatoms on the CD surface and several -OH and -COOH groups [26]. In particular, N-doping can provide additional amine and amide groups to CDs. As a result, nitrogen-doped CDs have a larger positive charge, which damages the membrane surface and increases the antibacterial efficacy [27]. In addition, functional composite films were prepared by adding N-TanCDs to the CS/Pul blend, and the mechanical properties, optical properties, surface functionalization, chemical composition, morphology, surface wettability, and water vapor permeability were analyzed. Then, the effectiveness of the N-TanCDs-added CS/Pul film was evaluated through the packaging of baked wheat bread.

Chitosan (at 1 % acetic acid, viscosity: 200–800 cP, 75–85 % degree of deacetylation based on viscosity, MW: 190,000–310,000) and pullulan was purchased from Daejung Chemicals & Metals Co., Ltd. (Siheung, Korea) and Korea Bio Polymer Co. Ltd. (Bucheon, Gyeonggi-do, Korea), respectively. BHI (brain heart infusion), nutrient broth, agar powder, and TSB (tryptic soy broth) were supplied by Duksan Pure Chemicals Co., Ltd. (Ansan, Korea). The Korean Collection for Type Culture supplied Listeria monocytogenes ATCC 15313, Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 23235, Salmonella enterica, and Escherichia coli O157: H7 ATCC 43895.

Nitrogen-doped tangerine peel-derived CDs (N-TanCDs) were synthesized following the hydrothermal method, as schematically shown in Fig. 1 [28]. Briefly, tangerine peels were washed and then kept overnight for drying in an oven at 45 °C. Then, 1.0 g of dried and ground Tan powder and 5 % (50 mg) urea (for nitrogen doping) were mixed with stirring in 50 mL of distilled water in a Teflon-lined autoclave (100 mL), reacted in a muffle furnace at 180 °C for 7 hours, and then the reactor was slowly cooled to room temperature. The resulting brown solution of Tangerine peel/nitrogen-doped CDs was then centrifuged for 15 min at 10,000 rpm to separate unwanted large particles of byproducts. Further purification was obtained by filtering the supernatant through a 25 mm diameter nylon filter (0.20 μm of pore size membrane filter). For comparison, pure TanCD without nitrogen doping was also prepared following a similar procedure. Finally, the obtained solution containing 4.0 mg/mL of CDs concentration was refrigerated at 4 °C for further analysis.

Chitosan/pullulan (CS/Pul) binary polymer-based films were developed by adopting a solution casting strategy. Regarding this, a 100 mL solution containing 1 % acetic acid was used to dissolve 2 g of chitosan, then 30 wt% glycerol (based on CS) was mixed and vigorously stirred at 80 °C for 2 h. Separately, 2 g of pullulan with 30 wt% glycerol (based on Pul) was dissolved in 100 mL of distilled water with stirring at 70 °C. Then, the CS and Pul solutions were mixed homogeneously with stirring for 1 h at 50 °C. Then, various concentrations of TanCD and N-TanCD (1 % and 3 % based on CS/Pul) were mixed with the above CS/Pul solution mixture under stirring at 50 °C for 1.0 h. The film solution was cast onto a glass plate (30 cm × 24 cm) coated with Teflon film and dried at room temperature (22 ± 2 °C) for 48 h. After complete drying, the films were collected and conditioned at 50 % RH, 25 °C for 48 h. For comparison, neat CS/Pul films were also engineered following the same route. The treated films were listed as CS/Pul, CS/Pul@TanCD1, CS/Pul@TanCD3, CS/Pul@N-TanCD1, and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 according to the type and concentration of CDs.

Characterization and analysis methods for the developed CDs and composite films, including shape and size, chemical composition, functional groups, color and optical characteristics, mechanical strength, water contact angle (WCA), water vapor permeability (WVP), surface morphology, and other investigations, are provided in the Supporting Information.

The fresh-baked bread was purchased at a local supermarket in Seoul. Bread slices (4 cm × 4 cm) were packed with the CS/Pul, CS/Pul@TanCD1, CS/Pul@TanCD3, CS/Pul@N-TanCD1, and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films (8 cm × 8 cm) and sealed with polyethylene sachet. As a control test, pieces of bread of the same size stored in a Petri dish and polyethylene wrap were used for comparison. All samples were stored in a chamber at 25 °C and 50 % RH for 12 days and visually monitored for quality degradation during storage. To evaluate the quality of packaged foods, moisture content, hardness, weight loss, and total viable count (TVC) were quantitatively measured. A Stoker (BagMixer® 400, Interscience, France) was used to homogenize 5 g of the tested bread samples in 45 mL of 0.1 % peptone water at 37 °C for 180 seconds at 100 rpm/min. Serially diluted homogeneous aliquots (1 mL) were then spread evenly onto 3 M™ Petri Film Counting Plates. TVC data based on viable colony counts were expressed as log CFU/g over a 48-h incubation period at 37 °C [29].

Three repetitions of each experiment were conducted, and the results are shown as mean ± standard deviation. The Duncan's Multiple Range Test was used to demonstrate significant differences (p < 0.05) between test groups after a one-way analysis of variance (ANOVA) was carried out using the SPSS statistical analysis system (SPSS Inc., Chicago, IL, USA).

The morphologies of TanCD and N-TanCD were observed using TEM ( Fig. 2a,b). TanCD and N-TanCD had a quasi-spherical shape, and the average size diameter was measured at approximately 9.2 nm and 8.4 nm, respectively. A Gaussian model-based histogram was plotted to determine the average size distribution of CDs (Fig. 2c,d) [12]. The particle diameters of TanCD and N-TanCD range from 4.5 to 14.8 nm and 4.2–15.1 nm, respectively. According to TEM observations, TanCD and N-TanCD had diameters in the quantum range and appeared to be monodisperse in various shapes and sizes [4].

FT-IR is used to determine the types of functional groups that are present on the CD surface, and the FT-IR spectra of TanCD and N-TanCD are shown in Fig. 2e. For both TanCD and N-TanCD, the IR absorption appeared around 3288 cm−1, with a small vibration peak at 1761 cm−1 and a peak at 1566 cm−1 due to the stretching vibration of the O-H/N-H group. (carbonyl group) and stretching polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons of CC bonds, each existing in the core of CDs [30]. In Fig. 2e, it can be clearly observed that the broad IR peaks of N-TanCD split to 3260 and 3157 cm−1 after nitrogen doping, which is attributed to the vibrations of -OH and -NH bonds and the shifts of the asymmetric and symmetric vibrations of C-O-C bond of the carboxylate group, which appeared as peaks at 1363 and 1056 cm−1, respectively [12]. The splitting of a single broad peak (TanCD) into two corresponding peaks of N-TanCD and the shift of other characteristic peaks confirm the doping of CD with nitrogen. Additionally, the peaks at 2931 and 1403 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching mode of the methylene group’s C-H bond. FTIR observations confirmed that the surfaces of TanCD and N-TanCD were composed of -COOH, -OH, CO, and CC groups, which were abundant on the surface. The presence of these active sites offers the possibility of surface modification of CDs with suitable heteroatoms or ligands and may increase their hydrophilicity and overall performance [11].

Brown solutions of TanCD and N-TanCD, produced by carbonizing Tan shell powder using a hydrothermal approach, emit blue fluorescence when exposed to 365 nm UV light (Fig. 2f,g, inset images) [12,31]. The phenomenon of emitting fluorescence emission upon exposure to UV irradiation is a characteristic property of CDs due to quantum confinement effects, as confirmed in previously published studies [28]. The UV–visible spectra (Fig. 2f,g) confirmed that both TanCDs and N-TanCDs have broad and sharp characteristic absorbance peaks centered at 291 and 290 nm, respectively, associated with the π-π* and n-π* transitions of the conjugated CC bonds and a carboxyl group (CO bonds) [31,32].

The fluorescence emission response of CDs generated varying excitation wavelengths within the range of 280–500 nm are shown in Fig. 2h, i. The photoluminescence emission spectra exhibit a notable wavelength dependency and shift to a longer region with rising excitation wavelengths. A similar excitation-dependent tunable fluorescence response was reported in previous investigations, which arises due to the varying shape and sizes of the synthesized CDs [12,31]. As shown in Fig. 2f, g, at the optimal excitation wavelength of 370 nm, TanCD and N-TanCD exhibit maximum fluorescence emission at 438 and 441 nm, respectively. UV-Vis and fluorescence response spectra show that similar optical properties were observed for both pristine TanCD and nitrogen-doped N-TanCD without significant differences. Only slight differences were observed in the emission and absorbance peak intensities. It is mostly accepted that the capabilities of CDs are mainly influenced by the carbon precursors that compose them [33]. For TanCD and N-TanCD, the core carbon structure doesn't change, with the Tan shell being the main carbon precursor. As a result, the carbon cores of TanCD and N-TanCD were identical, and the produced CDs had almost identical functional properties [33].

The antibacterial activity of TanCD and N-TanCD was determined against four bacterial strains (S. aureus, L. monocytogenes, S. enterica, and E. coli) and two fungal strains (A. flavus and P. chrysogenum) using the agar well diffusion assay ( Fig. 3a,b and Table S1 , Supporting Information). The inhibition zones of N-TanCD against S. aureus, L. monocytogenes, E. coli, and S. enterica were 9.7, 7.7, 4.1, and 6.8 mm, respectively. On the other hand, smaller inhibition zones were observed with TanCD for all four bacteria: L. monocytogenes (6.3 mm), E. coli (2.5 mm), S. enterica (5.2 mm), and S. aureus (5.1 mm). Zone of inhibition test results showed increased bactericidal activity of N-TanCD compared to TanCD against all four bacterial strains. Additionally, both CDs showed a higher bactericidal effect against Gram-positive bacteria (L. monocytogenes and S. aureus) than against Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli and S. enterica), which is due to differences in the structure of the cell walls of these bacteria [12].

Additionally, the antifungal efficacy of N-TanCD against both tested fungal strains was also improved after nitrogen doping (Fig. 3 c,d). After five days of incubation, the surface area of the control group was completely covered by the growth of A. flavus and P. chrysogenum fungal strains, whereas the group treated with N-TanCD showed a noticeable reduction in fungal growth. On the other hand, TanCD inhibited fungal growth less than N-TanCD.

Overall, the findings of the antibacterial and antifungal analyses validated that the antibacterial potential of CDs was meaningfully improved after doping them with nitrogen heteroatoms (N-TanCDs). Nitrogen doping increases the accessibility of electron-rich amide groups (-NH2) in N-TanCDs, and the resulting CD moieties provide more active sites on the surface to interact with microbial cell walls. Similar results were reported in previous investigations where doping CDs with heteroatoms improved the bioactive properties of CDs [2,5].

It is known that the antibacterial potential of CDs is mainly due to the production of reactive oxygen species (ROS) (HOO•, HO•, and O2 •−) promoted by amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl groups on the CDs’ surface [34]. CDs can react with the cell surface of microorganisms through ROS and affect the antioxidant or oxidative balance, eventually destroying biomolecules found in the cell wall and causing cell lysis [28,35]. Consequently, it can be said that synthetic N-TanCD has demonstrated promise in inhibiting the invasion of harmful and perishable microorganisms and reducing the barrier to the realization of new hybrid active food packaging substances.

The antioxidant action of TanCDs and N-TanCDs to neutralize or scavenge free radicals was determined utilizing the ABTS and DPPH assays [4], and the findings are depicted in Fig. 3e. The antioxidant activity of both CDs increased significantly as the concentration increased, and N-TanCD showed higher antioxidant activity than TanCD. In the case of pristine TanCD, the antioxidant efficacy towards ABTS was 99.8 ± 0.4 % at 100 μg/mL of concentration, but the antioxidant activity against DPPH was 87.6 ± 1.7 %. While for nitrogen-doped N-TanCD, the ABTS•+ free radical scavenging activity reached 100 ± 0.1 % at 75 μg/mL of concentration, while 91.7 ± 0.8 % DPPH• was scavenged at a concentration of 100 μg/mL. Doping nitrogen atoms into TanCD increased the availability of electron-rich amide groups (-NH2) apart from other active sites (-OH and -COOH) on the surface of N-TanCDs. Therefore, the ABTS•+ and DPPH• radicals neutralization improved by the transfer of H• from -NH2, -COOH, and -OH groups to DPPH-H and ABTS-H [28,36].

The FE-SEM micrographs of the surface and cross-section of CS/Pul@TanCD and CS/Pul@N-TanCD films are illustrated in Fig. 4a,b. The neat CS/Pul film indicates a compact, smooth and undisrupted surface appearance, indicating that the two polymers were homogeneously mixed [37]. Films containing TanCD and N-TanCD exhibited similar structural morphologies and smoother surfaces as the neat CS/Pul films, signifying that the compositing CD did not disturb the structural morphology of the films. The compatibility of CDs with CS/Pul polymer matrices may arise from the CDs’ hydrophilic nature and the abundance of amide, carboxyl, and hydroxyl functional groups on the surface of CDs [30]. As a result, the addition of CDs results in strong interactions with the polymer matrix between surface functional moieties through H-bonds, which are responsible for the homogeneous dispersion of CDs in the CS/Pul matrix [4]. The uneven distribution and good compatibility of CDs in CS/Pul@TanCD1, CS/Pul@TanCD3, CS/Pul@N/TanCD1, and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films are further confirmed by cross-sectional views showing intact morphology without holes, ruptures, cavities, or pores.

Elemental mapping obtained by EDS (energy dispersive X-ray) analysis showed that carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, the major elements of the CS/Pul matrix, were unvaryingly dispersed across the film surface (Fig. S1a-c , Supporting Information). The consistent distribution of nitrogen elements with increased concentration in the N-TanCD-added films (CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film) verified that the CDs were consistently dispersed in the fabricated film. The EDS analysis also validated the occurrence of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, the main elements of fabricated films. Nitrogen elements increased concentration for N-TanCD-added CS/Pul film, validating the integration of CDs with the fabricated film.

The actual images of the CS/Pul-based films are illustrated in Fig. 4c. The neat CS/Pul film showed an optically clear and transparent appearance. Incorporation of Tan-CD or N-TanCD into CS/Pul films results in a tan color, which further intensifies with increasing concentration of added CDs. The intensity of the tan color appears to be uniform, indicating a uniform distribution of the CDs contained in the fabricated films because of the compatibility of the CDs with the polymer matrices [4].

Additionally, the quantitative color values (L, a, b, and ΔE) of the CS/Pul-based films are shown in Table 1. The CS/Pul film with a lightness value (L) of 89.1 appeared to be highly transparent. The transparency of the neat CS/Pul was slightly decreased upon the inclusion of TanCD or N-TanCD, which resulted in a decrease in brightness (L) and subsequent increase in yellowness (b) and redness (a) of the composite films. The results showed that loading the CD significantly (p < 0.05) increased the film’s total color difference (ΔE). The addition of both TanCD and N-TanCD had a moderate effect on light transmission (visible region) through CS/Pul-based composite films (Fig. 4d). As can be seen from the films’ light transmittance results, the neat CS/Pul film signifying high light transmittance across the recorded wavelengths (200–800 nm), but the light transmittance was slightly reduced for films added with TanCD or N-TanCD. Light transmittance at T660 (660 nm) and T280 (280 nm) was used to measure the transparency and UV-barrier capabilities of the film. The findings are displayed in Table 1. The T660 and T280 of the neat CS/Pul film were 87.9 % and 6.3 %, respectively, signifying that the CS/Pul film is highly transparent with moderate UV-locking abilities. However, the addition of TanCD and N-TanCD slightly reduced the transparency of the composite film (decreasing the T660 from 87.9 % to 80.8 %) while significantly increasing the UV-blocking ability (decreasing the T280 from 6.3 % to 0.1 %). The UV-barrier property of the CD-loaded films was more clearly observed in the films’ light transmittance spectra (Fig. 4d,e). The UV blocking ability of the composite film increased with increasing CD concentration, and the N-doped CDs (N-TanCDs) were more effective in increasing UV blocking properties than the pristine CDs (TanCDs). In particular, the CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film was shown to almost completely block UV-A (320–400 nm) and UV-B (280–320 nm) light. Biopolymer films loaded with CDs have excellent UV protection due to the fact that CDs reflect or absorb UV radiations and prevent their transmission. Also, it is believed that the UV-blocking ability is further enhanced due to the strong UV-blocking potential of the aromatic polyphenol component comprised in CDs prepared from tangerine peels.

FTIR spectra were assessed to investigate the chemical interactions between the functional groups of CDs and the polymer matrix of CS/Pul-based films, and the outcomes are illustrated in Fig. 5a. All CS/Pul-based films exhibit a broad peak around 3214–3438 cm−1 matching to the O-H/N-H vibrational stretching mode [36]. The IR absorption peaks at 2931, 1645, and 1524 cm−1 allocated to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching of the C-H bond, stretching of amide I (CO), and amide II (NH bending) asymmetric stretching, respectively [38]. In CS/Pul films incorporated with Tan/CD and N-TanCD, the distinctive peak of CO was seen around 1724 cm−1. The peaks at 1465 and 1342 cm−1 confirm the bending of CH2 and the CH3 group's symmetrical deformation, and the IR peaks at 1187 cm−1 and 1251 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C-O-C group’s stretching vibration; the peaks appeared around 1011, 1088, and 864 cm−1 indicated to the C-O group’s stretching [19]. The absorption in 757 and 921 cm−1 corresponded to the occurrence of α-1, 4-D-glucosidic linkage and α-1, 6-D-glucosidic linkage, respectively [19]. Tan/CDs and N-TanCDs incorporated CS/Pul@TanCD and CS/Pul@N/Tan-CD films showed analogous major distinctive IR peaks related to the CS/Pul film with marginal differences in intensities or slight shifting. This shift in peak position suggests that intermolecular H-bond interactions occurred between the N-H, O-H, and -COOH groups of CDs and CS/Pul polymers [19,36].

Additionally, XPS responses were recorded to examine the surface chemical composition of CS/Pul and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films. Both films tested confirmed the occurrence of the main components oxygen, carbon and nitrogen, indicated by their binding energies at 285.0, 531.1 and 400.0 eV (Fig. 5b). The high-resolution C1s spectra of both films revealed four slitted peaks with the bonding sites of C-C/C-H with the sp2 orbital indicating the peak at 283.8 eV of binding energy (Fig. 5c). The bonding structures of C-N/CC, C-O, and CO (carbonyl) or O-C-O are assigned to the other split peaks at 285.3, 286.6, and 287.5 eV, respectively [14,39]. Additionally, the N 1 s spectra can be split into three peaks corresponding to the binding energies of 398.1, 399.2 eV, and 401.0, indicating the existence of C-N-C, N-C, and N-H/N-(CH3)3 + groups, respectively (Fig. 5d) [4,30]. Fig. 5e shows that O 1 s spectra by splitting into three peaks at ∼530.1 eV, 531.4 eV, and 532.4 eV of binding energies, validating the presence of C-O-H/C-O-C, CO, and O-C-O bonds [40].

The mechanical properties of CS/Pul-based films, such as TS (tensile strength), EM (elastic modulus), and EB (elongation at break), are cataloged in Table 2. When CDs were added, the thickness of the film increased notably (p < 0.05), which was because of the increased solid content by the loading of the CDs. In addition, integrating TanCD and N-TanCD affected the mechanical characteristics of the CS/PUL-based active packaging films. The TS, EM, and EB of the neat CS/Pul film were 69.3 MPa, 2.3 GPa, and 4.5 %, respectively, signifying it is comparatively strong and rigid with less flexibility. The loading of 3 wt% TanCD and N-TanCD slightly increased the TS, EM, and EB of the film, indicating increased strength, stiffness, and flexibility of the film. Here, it is believed that CDs served as a plasticizer to increase flexibility by improving the biopolymer molecule’s molecular mobility in the film matrix [41].

Interestingly, adding the CDs notably increased (p < 0.05) the strength of the film. The degree of increase was proportional to the amount of CD added, and N-TanCD showed a greater increase than TanCD. The increase in TS in the composite films is probably due to the interfacial interactions between the CD particles and the polymer matrix, as well as the H-bond interactions between the CS/Pul chains and the CD functional groups [36]. The mechanical characteristics of biopolymer-based films have been reported to increase when incorporating hydrophilic CDs, conceivably due to intermolecular interactions among nanofillers and the polymer matrix [4,28].

The WVP of the film was also affected by the loading of the CDs (Table 2). The WVP of the neat CS/Pul film was 7.4×10−10 g.m/m2.Pa.s, which was comparable to the previously published nanofiller-added biopolymer-based composite films [4,42]. The insertion of CDs, except for 1 wt% TanCD, significantly increased (p < 0.05) WVP of the film. It is alleged that the decrease in vapor barrier properties (i.e., increase in WVP) of the CS/Pul film is due to the hydrophilic nature of CDs [43,44].

The hydrophilic nature of the films determined by WCA was also affected similarly to WVP (Table 2). The WCA of the neat CS/Pul film was 60.3°, indicating that the film had a hydrophilic surface. The WCA of the film decreased notably (p < 0.05) with the incorporation of CDs, which also depicted the hydrophilic nature of the CDs. It is interesting to note that adding N-TanCDs lowered the WCA of the film more than adding TanCDs, indirectly indicating that N-doped TanCDs are more hydrophilic than pristine TanCDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antioxidant and antimicrobial activity of the films
ce:section.ce:para: The antioxidant potential of the CS/Pul-based film was estimated by determining the radical neutralizing activity of DPPH• and ABTS•+, and the results are shown in Fig. 6a. The DPPH and ABTS antioxidant activities of neat CS/Pul films were recorded as 11.4 % and 24.2 %, respectively, due to the electron-rich -NH2 chitosan groups present in the binary polymer mixture. However, the incorporation of TanCD and N-TanCD into the CS/Pul film notably increased (p < 0.05) the radical neutralizing activity of the film, which was ascribed to the surface electron-rich hydroxyl, carboxyl and amide groups of the CDs. Additionally, the CS/Pul@TanCD3 and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films containing 3 wt% CDs exhibited ABTS neutralization rates of 82.3 % and 91.6 %, while DPPH radical scavenging rates were 55.9 % and 62.6 %, respectively. The higher antioxidant activity of the CS/Pul@TanCD3 and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films can be documented to the high radical scavenging potential of the CDs (Fig. 3e) enclosed with active sites such as -OH, -CO, and -NH2 groups that scavenge free radicals by blocking free radical chain reactions [30,45].

The bactericidal action of the CS/Pul-based films was evaluated against common foodborne bacteria such as E. coli and L. monocytogenes, and the outcomes are illustrated in Fig. 6b, c. The neat CS/Pul film displayed significant antibacterial action against both test bacteria, which was because of the antibacterial efficacy of chitosan [46]. However, when the CDs were loaded into the CS/Pul film, the antibacterial efficacy of the film increased more significantly. The antibacterial activity of the CS/Pul-based composite films containing CDs was impacted by the concentration of CDs added and the type of CDs. In particular, in the case of CS/Pul@TanCD3 and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films, after 12 hours of incubation, the growth of L. monocytogenes was decreased by 8.1 and 8.9 log CFU/mL, respectively, and the growth of E. coli was suppressed by 7.5 and 8.4 log CFU/mL in comparison to the control group. As confirmed in the sterilization test results of TanCD and N-TanCD (Fig. 3a,b), the CD-loaded films showed more effective antibacterial potential against Gram-positive bacteria than Gram-negative bacteria.

Sliced bread was packaged with CS/Pul-based film and stored at 25°C and 50 % relative humidity for 12 days ( Fig. 7a). In bread samples stored in commercial polyethylene packaging, the fungal infection began to appear on the fourth day of storage and reached full growth by the eighth day of packaging. In the case of unpackaged bread (opened in a Petri dish), no mold growth was observed because all the moisture needed for mold growth had evaporated. Inversely, mold invasion was delayed until 8 days in bread samples packaged with the neat CS/Pul film, and no mold growth was noticed in the bread packaged with CD-added CS/Pul films even after 12 days of storage. The mold growth inhibition effect of CD-containing CS/Pul film is because of the antibacterial effect of chitosan and CDs of the packaging film.

In addition, moisture content, hardness, and weight loss are significant factors that determine the quality of bread and contribute to the freshness and physical stability of bread [47]. Moisture content determines the total moisture content of fresh food, which affects the chemical and physical activities of the food [48]. The moisture content of the bread samples decreased steadily over the storage period, with the greatest decrease observed in the unpackaged bread samples (Fig. 7b). Additionally, the moisture content reduction rate of packaged bread was lower than that of unpackaged bread, and CS/Pul composite films with added CDs were similar to or superior to PE film in maintaining the moisture content of bread. The weight loss of bread during storage is shown in Fig. 7c. There was an opposite trend to the change in moisture content since the weight loss of the bread during storage was mainly due to the decrease in moisture content. In particular, after 12 days of storage, the unpackaged bread showed a weight loss of 41.0 %, while the weight loss of bread packaged with the CS/Pul@TanCD3 and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films was 19.7 % and 17.9 %, respectively.

The firmness or hardness of bread is an important aesthetic and textural characteristic because the harder the bread, the greater the force required to compress it [49]. In general, the hardness of bread is greatly affected by its moisture content. As shown in Fig. 7d, the hardness of the bread increased as the storage period increased, and the rate of change in hardness differed depending on the packaging films. Bread packaged with CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film maintained moisture content inside the package, resulting in a minimal increase in hardness compared to bread packaged with other film samples. Increased hardness of bread is associated with bread staling that happens throughout storage and comprises phase transitions that change the starch structure of the bread. In particular, the starch aging process, which involves the recrystallization of amylopectin and amylose, helps remove moisture from the gluten network and hardens the crumb structure, resulting in harder slices of bread [50].

Microorganisms in packaged bread, as determined by total viable colony count (TVC), increased during storage, as shown in Fig. 7e. The initial TVC of bread was 0.95 Log CFU/g but increased quickly during storage, reaching 7.3, 6.1, and 5.7 Log CFU/g after 12 days for bread packaged in polyethylene film, unpacked, and neat CS/Pul film, respectively. On the other hand, the increase in TVC values was noticeably delayed in bread packaged with CS/Pul@TanCD3 and CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 films. In particular, the TVC of bread packaged with CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film was less than 3.7 Log CFU/g even after 12 days of storage. The prevention of TVC increase in bread by CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film is due to the increase of amide functional groups in nitrogen-doped N-TanCD (excluding hydroxyl and carboxyl groups), which increases the antibacterial, antioxidant and UV properties of CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film. The enhanced functional properties of CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film reduce microbial invasion into packaged bread, maintaining moisture content and hardness, ultimately slowing down weight loss and maintaining the original quality of packaged products. The results confirmed that CS/Pul@N-TanCD film could be used as an active packaging material to extend the shelf life of food.

In this study, nitrogen-doped Tan shell-derived CDs were used as activators to prepare CS/Pul-based active packaging films with improved physicochemical, antioxidant, antibacterial, and light-shielding properties. Food processing byproducts, such as tangerine peel, were found to be environmentally safe and more economical carbon precursors for CD synthesis. The CS/Pul composite film with the addition of N-TanCD not only showed increased chemical and physical properties but also showed efficient functional properties such as excellent antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria, excellent antioxidant properties, and UV-blocking properties. Packaging tests conducted to preserve sliced bread showed that CS/Pul@N-TanCD3 film efficiently retards the invasion of foodborne pathogens, preserving aesthetic value and quality during storage at 4 °C for 12 days, thus extending the shelf life of bread. Therefore, it is expected that CDs can be produced using food processing byproducts such as tangerine peels or food waste and used as functional fillers to create eco-friendly and highly functional active packaging materials, thereby efficiently utilizing natural resources and achieving sustainable development.
